method_atom: an analysis of how spatial dimensionality controls the emergence and robustness of collective order in a system of many interacting units, where fluctuations grow stronger in lower dimensions and can destroy true long-range order while still permitting a distinct 'quasi-long-range' ordered phase; at the marginal (two-dimensional) case a topological ordering transition separates a low-parameter phase with algebraically decaying correlations and nonzero collective rigidity from a high-parameter disordered phase, with the interplay between full ordering and this reduced rigidity strongly modified by finite system size.
object_atom: an interacting two-dimensional Bose fluid realized as ultra-cold atomic gases confined to a planar geometry, exhibiting Bose-Einstein condensation and superfluidity at low but nonzero temperature.
brief_reason: The transferable part is the dimensionality-dependent fluctuation/ordering machinery (loss of true long-range order, surviving quasi-long-range order and collective rigidity, marginal 2d transition, finite-size effects); the domain-specific part is the ultra-cold 2d Bose atomic gas with condensation and superfluidity.
